1: Dinah, the daughter Leah had borne to Jacob, went out to visit the women of the land — Dinah’s going out to the women of Canaan is presented without moral comment; visiting neighboring women was ordinary life. The vulnerability of the covenant community’s members when they “go out” into the surrounding world is a recurring concern. The church lives “in the world but not of the world” (John 17:11–16) — present in the surrounding culture, not cloistered, but exposed to its dangers. Christ prays not that the Father would “take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one” (John 17:15). The covenant community’s exposure to the world is not the problem; the world’s violation of the covenant community is the sin.
2: Shechem son of Hamor… saw her, he took her and raped her — the assault on Dinah is blunt in the Hebrew: he took, he lay with, he violated her. The violation of the innocent by power is the fundamental form of sin — the strong taking from the weak what they have no right to take. The entire passion narrative shows Christ “taken” by those in power (Matthew 26:50: “they came forward, seized Jesus”), unjustly treated. But Christ takes on himself not only the suffering of the violated but also the guilt of the violators: “he was pierced for our transgressions” (Isaiah 53:5). Shechem’s sin and its consequences become the type of sin’s injury — both to the victim and through the cycle of revenge it generates — that Christ bears to break.
3: His heart was drawn to Dinah; he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to her — Shechem’s tender words after the assault. The violence followed by soft words is the pattern of exploitation that uses emotional language to reframe wrongdoing. The prophets name this precisely: false prophets who “heal the wound of my people lightly, saying ‘Peace, peace’ when there is no peace” (Jeremiah 6:14). The tender speech that does not undo the harm it attempts to cover is the religious and moral danger that Christ names: “Watch out for false prophets. They come to you in sheep’s clothing” (Matthew 7:15). The violence of sin cannot be made right by tender words; only the blood of the covenant achieves genuine reconciliation (Hebrews 9:22).
4: Shechem said to his father Hamor, “Get me this girl as my wife” — the demand for marriage after the assault. Shechem wants covenant relationship (marriage) after covenant violation (rape). The desire to regularize sin through formal relationship — to make right through structure what was wrong in act — is the human attempt to substitute form for genuine repentance. Christ refuses the form without the substance: “Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven” (Matthew 7:21). The confession of “Lord” without the transformation of life is the Shechem-pattern: claiming covenant relationship without covenant transformation.
5: When Jacob heard that his daughter Dinah had been defiled, his sons were in the fields; so he said nothing about it until they came home — Jacob’s silence. The father who knows of his daughter’s assault and says nothing — waiting for the sons — is a disturbing image of covenant leadership that defers action. The contrast with the divine Father: God does not wait and defer when his people are violated; “he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help” (Psalms 22:24). Christ acts immediately when the suffering cry reaches him: “he was moved with compassion” (Matthew 9:36; Genesis 14:14; Genesis 15:32) — the opposite of the silence that defers action.
6: Shechem’s father Hamor went out to talk with Jacob — Hamor’s diplomatic approach. The attempt to negotiate a settlement for an act that requires justice rather than negotiation is the misapplication of diplomacy to moral wrong. The NT is clear that justice cannot be bartered away: “Do not take revenge, my dear friends, but leave room for God’s wrath, for it is written: ‘It is mine to avenge; I will repay,’ says the Lord” (Romans 12:19). Christ does not negotiate sin away; he pays sin’s penalty in full. The cross is not a settlement but a satisfaction: “he is the propitiation for our sins” (1 John 2:2 ESV) — the sin is not managed but absorbed and paid.
7: Jacob’s sons had come in from the fields; they were shocked and furious, because Shechem had done a disgraceful thing in Israel — the brothers’ righteous outrage at the violation of their sister. The anger is called righteous by the narrative’s framing: the act was genuinely “a disgraceful thing in Israel” (nebalah be-yisra’el), a phrase the OT uses for acts that violate the covenant community’s order (Deuteronomy 22:21; 2 Samuel 13:12). Christ is the one who takes righteous anger seriously: “he made a whip out of cords, and drove all from the temple courts” (John 2:15). The indignation at genuine injustice is not sin but the proper moral response. The brothers’ anger is not wrong; their response (vv25–29) exceeds justice into vengeance.
8: Hamor said to them, “My son Shechem has his heart set on your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife” — Hamor’s request reframes the assault as romance: Shechem’s “heart is set on” Dinah, as if the violence were simply ardent love. The narrative’s earlier precision (“took her and raped her,” v2) is the counterweight to Hamor’s romantic framing. The reframing of sin as something other than what it is — the softening language that makes wrongdoing palatable — is what Paul calls “exchanging the truth about God for a lie” (Romans 1:25). Christ insists on naming sin as sin: “For out of the heart come evil thoughts — murder, adultery, sexual immorality” (Matthew 15:19). The naming of sin truthfully is the first step of the gospel.
9: “Intermarry with us; give us your daughters and take our daughters for yourselves” — Hamor’s proposal of intermarriage. The absorption of the covenant community into the surrounding culture through intermarriage was consistently the path that diluted the covenant (as later in Israel’s history — Deuteronomy 7:3–4; Ezra 9–10). The covenant community’s distinctiveness is essential to its witness. Paul in 2 Corinthians 6:14–18: “Do not be yoked together with unbelievers… What does a believer have in common with an unbeliever?” The distinctiveness that Hamor’s proposal would dissolve is preserved in the church by the covenant identity that Christ gives: “a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation” (1 Peter 2:9).
10: “You can settle among us; the land is open to you. Live in it, trade in it, and acquire property in it” — the generous-sounding offer: the covenant community is invited to assimilate. The world’s consistent offer to the covenant community is assimilation in exchange for comfort and security: “live among us, trade, acquire” — the material benefits of belonging. Christ describes the alternative: “Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth… But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven” (Matthew 6:19–20). The offer of land, trade, and property is not inherently wrong, but when it comes at the cost of covenant identity, the covenant community must say no. The covenant-bearer’s relationship with surrounding culture requires wisdom, not assimilation.
11: Shechem said, “Let me find favor in your eyes, and I will give you whatever you ask” — Shechem’s personal appeal alongside his father’s diplomatic proposal. “Let me find favor in your eyes” is the grace-language of the covenant applied by an outsider seeking covenant inclusion. The genuine desire for covenant belonging that Shechem expresses — however compromised by the context — is the type of the Gentile longing to be included in God’s covenant. Paul’s gospel is the legitimate fulfillment of this longing: “you who once were far away have been brought near by the blood of Christ” (Ephesians 2:13). Christ is the one who genuinely opens covenant inclusion to all who seek it — without the violence and deception that mark this episode.
12: “Make the price for the bride and the gift as great as you like, and I’ll pay whatever you ask” — Shechem offers unlimited bride-price. The willingness to pay any price for the beloved is a genuine virtue that the covenant context has corrupted: the wrongdoing preceded the negotiation. But the pattern of paying any price for the bride is the type of Christ’s atoning purchase: “you were bought at a price” (1 Corinthians 6:20; Genesis 7:23); “the church of God, which he bought with his own blood” (Acts 20:28). The difference: Christ’s payment purchases what he has rightful claim to; Shechem’s payment attempts to purchase what his violence forfeited. The bride-price of the gospel is the blood of Christ, not silver or gold (1 Peter 1:18–19).
13: Jacob’s sons replied deceitfully as they spoke to Shechem and his father Hamor — the deception-pattern continues into the next generation. The sons of Jacob, like their father (who deceived Laban and Isaac), respond to Shechem with deceit. The moral inheritance of deception — the sin of the father visited on the children (Exodus 20:5) — is the OT’s realistic account of how sin propagates through families. Christ comes to break this generational pattern: “everyone who sins is a slave to sin” (John 8:34) — but “if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed” (John 8:36). The liberation from the inherited patterns of deception and violence is the gospel’s specific promise to those entangled in generational sin.
14: “We can’t give our sister to a man who is not circumcised. That would be a disgrace to us” — the brothers use circumcision as the pretext for their plan. Circumcision as the covenant sign being weaponized as a tool of deception is a profound corruption of the covenant. Paul in Galatians 5:6 strips circumcision of its power as a legal requirement: “neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value” — what matters is “faith expressing itself through love.” The brothers use circumcision as a tool of violence; Paul says circumcision used as a tool of coercion is no better (Galatians 5:2–3). The covenant sign is meant to mark covenant belonging, not to serve as a weapon in human schemes.
15: “We will enter into an agreement with you on one condition only: that you become like us by circumcising all your males” — the demand for circumcision as the price of covenant inclusion. Simeon and Levi use the covenant requirement as a pretext — not as a genuine invitation to covenant membership but as a means of incapacitation. The use of religious requirements as instruments of power and manipulation is what the NT calls “burdening with a yoke that neither we nor our ancestors have been able to bear” (Acts 15:10). Christ does not use covenant requirements as instruments of manipulation: “Come to me… my yoke is easy and my burden is light” (Matthew 11:28–30). The covenant entrance that Christ offers is genuine, not a trap.
16: “Then we will give you our daughters and take your daughters for ourselves. We’ll settle among you and become one people with you” — the brothers’ false promise of unity. The promise of becoming “one people” that will not be kept is a counterfeit of the genuine unity that Christ brings. John 17:21: “that all of them may be one, Father, just as you are in me and I am in you — so that the world may believe that you have sent me.” The genuine oneness of the covenant community in Christ is the fulfillment of the hope for unity that these negotiations corrupt. The brothers’ false “one people” is overtaken by Christ’s genuine “one flock, one shepherd” (John 10:16).
17: “But if you will not agree to be circumcised, we’ll take our sister and go” — the ultimatum. The covenant community’s ultimatum to the surrounding world — accept our terms or we depart — is a legitimate posture when the terms are genuine covenant rather than a trap. Paul’s principle: “What do I have to do with judging those outside? Are you not to judge those inside? God will judge those outside” (1 Corinthians 5:12–13). The covenant community does not force its terms on the surrounding world; it maintains its distinctiveness and departs when its integrity is threatened. Here the ultimatum is a deception; but the underlying principle of covenant distinctiveness is genuine.
18: Their proposal seemed good to Hamor and his son Shechem — the Shechemites accept the terms. The acceptance of the covenant requirement (circumcision) without genuine covenant commitment — for strategic rather than faith reasons (v23: “won’t their livestock become ours?”) — is the type of the false conversion that takes the form of covenant without the substance. James 2:19: “even the demons believe — and shudder.” The mere acceptance of external religious requirements without the transformation of the heart is what Paul calls “a form of godliness but denying its power” (2 Timothy 3:5). True covenant membership is circumcision of the heart (Romans 2:29; Deuteronomy 10:16), not merely the flesh.
19: The young man lost no time in doing what they said, because he was delighted with Jacob’s daughter — Shechem’s eager compliance, motivated by desire for Dinah rather than covenant faith. The action without the right motivation is the type of religious observance without genuine faith: “These people honor me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me” (Matthew 15:8, citing Isaiah 29:13). The speed of Shechem’s compliance — “lost no time” — is motivated by self-interest (wanting Dinah) not by covenant belonging. Christ sees through external compliance to the motive: “Man looks at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart” (1 Samuel 16:7, cited in context of Christ’s insight: John 2:25: “he knew what was in each person”).
20: Hamor and his son Shechem went to the gate of their city to speak to the men of their city — the city gate as the place of public deliberation and covenant-making. The gate is the place of law and community decision (Ruth 4:1–11; Deuteronomy 21:19). The covenant negotiation being conducted publicly at the gate — the legitimate procedure — is being used here for a scheme that is not legitimate. The formal structures of justice and community deliberation — good in themselves — can be corrupted by those who use them for unjust ends. Christ’s trial before Pilate is the supreme example: legitimate judicial procedure (the governor, the legal accusation, the public hearing) used to execute an unjust verdict on the one who had done nothing wrong (Luke 23:4: “I find no basis for a charge against this man”).
21: “These men are friendly toward us. Let them live in our land” — Hamor’s community speech reframes the covenant negotiation as straightforward friendship and mutual benefit. The language of friendliness and mutual gain that conceals the full picture — Shechem’s assault, the brothers’ deception — is the selective truth-telling that the NT consistently condemns: “speaking the truth in love” (Ephesians 4:15) requires the whole truth, not the convenient portion. Christ promises the Spirit of Truth who will “guide you into all the truth” (John 16:13) — the complete, not selective, disclosure that covenant relationship requires.
22: “The men will agree to live with us as one people only on the condition that our males be circumcised, as they themselves are” — Hamor reports the condition honestly to his city. At least at this level — in reporting the terms to his own people — Hamor is straightforward. The partial honesty that operates within one’s own community while being deceptive toward the covenant community is the normal operating mode of the world’s engagement with the church. Christ calls his disciples to a higher standard: “Let your ‘Yes’ be ‘Yes,’ and your ‘No,’ ‘No’” (Matthew 5:37) — consistency in truth-telling across all relationships, not merely within the in-group.
23: “Won’t their livestock, their property and all their other animals become ours?” — the economic motivation for the Shechemites’ agreement: they calculate that accepting circumcision will eventually transfer the covenant family’s wealth to them. The acceptance of covenant forms for economic benefit — the prosperity gospel inverted — is the constant temptation to use religious allegiance for material gain. Paul warns Timothy: “godliness with contentment is great gain” (1 Timothy 6:6) but those who think “godliness is a means to financial gain” (v5) are corrupted. Christ explicitly refused material benefit as a motivation for following him: “Do not work for food that spoils, but for food that endures to eternal life” (John 6:27).
24: All the men who went out of the city gate agreed… and every male in the city was circumcised — the mass circumcision of the Shechemites. The entire male population submitting to the covenant sign without covenant faith is the type of the mass religious conformity that the prophets consistently condemned: “This people draws near to me with their mouth and honors me with their lips, while their hearts are far from me” (Isaiah 29:13; Matthew 15:8). The circumcision of the flesh without the circumcision of the heart (Jeremiah 4:4; Romans 2:29) is the perpetual religious failure. Christ comes to accomplish what mass circumcision cannot: “In him you were also circumcised with a circumcision not performed by human hands… the circumcision of Christ” (Colossians 2:11).
25: Three days later, while all of them were still in pain, two of Jacob’s sons, Simeon and Levi, took their swords and attacked the unsuspecting city, killing every male — the massacre on the third day. Simeon and Levi exploit the incapacitation that the covenant sign has caused to carry out their revenge. The perversion of the covenant sign — used to make victims of those who accepted it — is one of the darkest actions in Genesis. The righteous anger (v7) becomes righteous murder by the logic of revenge. Paul’s warning: “Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good” (Romans 12:21). Christ overcomes the evil of the cross not by returning violence but by absorbing it and transforming it through the resurrection. The Simeon-and-Levi response is the way of the world; the cross is the way of God.
26: They put Hamor and his son Shechem to the sword and took Dinah from Shechem’s house and left — Dinah is retrieved from Shechem’s house — she had apparently been kept there. The liberation of Dinah is the only righteous element of the brothers’ action; the slaughter of the entire city goes far beyond what justice required. Christ’s liberation is precise and proportionate: he bears exactly the penalty that sin requires (no more, no less) and liberates exactly those for whom he died. The liberation of Dinah at the cost of disproportionate violence is the contrast with the liberation of sinners at the precisely proportionate cost of Christ’s atoning death: “the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God” (1 Peter 3:18).
27: The sons of Jacob came upon the dead and looted the city — the wider looting carried out by the other brothers after Simeon and Levi’s attack. The violence expands beyond the two initiators: the other sons join in the looting. Sin consistently spreads: the initial violence creates an environment in which further sin becomes acceptable. “A little yeast leavens the whole batch of dough” (Galatians 5:9; 1 Corinthians 5:6). The same principle that Paul applies to the corrupting influence of false teaching applies to the corrupting influence of violence: once begun, it spreads through the whole. Christ alone can stop the spread: “The blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin” (1 John 1:7).
28: They seized their flocks and herds and donkeys and everything else in the city and out in the fields — the systematic plundering of the Shechemites’ property. The taking of property from those who were killed follows the logic of warfare-as-plunder that runs through the ancient world. The contrast with Christ: at the cross, the soldiers divided his garments (Matthew 27:35, fulfilling Psalms 22:18) — the covenant-bearer’s property distributed among his enemies. Where Jacob’s sons took the property of those they killed, the soldiers took Christ’s property while killing him. The reversal is the shape of the cross: not the victorious one who plunders, but the innocent one who is plundered for the sins of others.
29: They carried off all their wealth and all their women and children, taking as plunder everything in the houses — the enslavement of the Shechemite women and children. The victors who enslave the defeated is the ancient world’s normal outcome of warfare; the moral catastrophe is presented without whitewash. Jacob’s rebuke (v30) and his deathbed curse of Simeon and Levi (Genesis 49:5–7) show the narrative’s moral judgment on the action. Christ comes to reverse the victor-enslaves-the-defeated logic: “For the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Mark 10:45). The ransom liberates the enslaved; the covenant-bearer’s death sets free those whom violence had captivated.
30: Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “You have brought trouble on me by making me obnoxious to the Canaanites and Perizzites” — Jacob’s rebuke focuses on the practical danger to the covenant family (the surrounding nations may retaliate) rather than the moral wrong done to the Shechemites. Jacob’s self-centered rebuke contrasts with God’s comprehensive moral vision. The prophets will later indict Israel’s leaders for the same failure: rebuking wrongdoing not because it was wrong but because it was inconvenient: “Woe to those who call evil good and good evil” (Isaiah 5:20). Christ’s moral vision is comprehensive: he weeps over Jerusalem not because it is dangerous for him but because of what Jerusalem is doing to itself (Luke 19:41–44). The covenant-bearer who sees the full moral picture rather than the convenient one.
31: But they replied, “Should he have treated our sister like a prostitute?” — Simeon and Levi’s final word, which the narrative gives them without refutation. Their defense is morally coherent — Shechem’s treatment of Dinah was genuinely wrong — even if their response was disproportionate. The unanswered question that closes Genesis 34 is the OT’s honest reckoning with the gap between moral outrage and moral response: righteous anger does not automatically produce righteous action. Christ addresses this gap: “In your anger do not sin” (Ephesians 4:26, citing Psalms 4:4). The anger at genuine injustice is legitimate; the channeling of that anger into violence that exceeds justice is the failure. The brothers’ unanswered question awaits the answer that the cross provides: injustice is answered not by counter-violence but by the bearing of its full weight.
